content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 4 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 4 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 4:1 -- "regard us / consider us / look at us" and "servants / mere servants"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Thus let a man consider us as servants of Christos and stewards of [the] mysteries of God" | logizomai as "consider"; hyperetas as "servants" |
| LOGOS | "This is how one should regard us, as servants of Christ and stewards of the mysteries of God" | logizomai as "regard"; same structure |
| PNEUMA | "So look at Apollos and me as mere servants of Christ who have been put in charge of explaining God's mysteries" | Adds "Apollos and me" (names Paul); "mere servants"; "put in charge of explaining" for oikonomous |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Regard" is appropriately formal for logizomai. PNEUMA's addition of "Apollos and me" names who is implied but not stated. "Mere servants" adds a qualifier. Most significantly, "put in charge of explaining" for oikonomos (steward) reduces the role -- a steward manages and dispenses, not merely explains.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:3 -- "human court / human day / human authority"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it is a smallest [thing] that I be judged by you or by a human day" | anthropines hemeras literally as "human day" |
| LOGOS | "it is a very small thing that I should be judged by you or by any human court" | anthropines hemeras as "human court" (interpretive) |
| PNEUMA | "it matters very little how I might be evaluated by you or by any human authority" | anthropines hemeras as "human authority"; krino as "evaluated" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Human court" correctly interprets the idiom anthropines hemeras ("human day"), which refers to a day of human judgment -- hence, a court or tribunal. EMET's literal "human day" is opaque in English. PNEUMA's "human authority" is acceptable but less specific.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's literal rendering noted for transparency.

---

## 4:4 -- "acquitted / justified / prove I'm right"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am not justified by this; but the [one] judging me is [the] Lord" | dikaioo as "justified" |
| LOGOS | "I am not thereby acquitted. It is the Lord who judges me" | dikaioo as "acquitted" |
| PNEUMA | "that doesn't prove I'm right. It is the Lord himself who will examine me and decide" | dikaioo as "prove I'm right"; adds "himself," "examine," "decide" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Acquitted" correctly captures the forensic sense of dikaioo in this context -- Paul is speaking of judicial verdict, not moral justification. PNEUMA's "prove I'm right" is too colloquial for a legal metaphor and adds "examine and decide" beyond the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:5 -- "commendation/praise" for epainos

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "then the praise will come to each from God" | epainos as "praise" |
| LOGOS | "Then each one will receive his commendation from God" | epainos as "commendation" |
| PNEUMA | "Then God will give to each one whatever praise is due" | Adds "whatever...is due" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Commendation" for epainos captures the evaluative sense better than "praise" (which in English often implies worship). PNEUMA's "whatever praise is due" adds a qualifying phrase not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:6 -- "Do not go beyond what is written" and "puffed up"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that in us you may learn the [saying] 'not beyond what has been written,' that you may not be puffed up, one on behalf of the one against the other" | Preserves Greek structure; physiomai as "puffed up" |
| LOGOS | "that you may learn by us the meaning of the saying, 'Do not go beyond what is written,' that none of you may be puffed up in favor of one against another" | Adds "the meaning of the saying" for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "If you pay attention to what I have quoted from the Scriptures, you won't be proud of one of your leaders at the expense of another" | Major restructuring; "proud" for physiomai; "leaders" adds interpretation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The obscure phrase "not beyond what is written" is preserved as a citation (likely a known saying). LOGOS's framing with "the meaning of the saying" aids comprehension. PNEUMA's restructuring loses the quotation form and interprets "puffed up" as merely "proud," missing the inflation metaphor Paul repeatedly employs.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:7 -- "who sees anything different in you / who distinguishes you / what gives you the right"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For who distinguishes you? And what do you have that you did not receive?" | diakrino as "distinguishes" |
| LOGOS | "For who sees anything different in you? What do you have that you did not receive?" | diakrino as "sees anything different in" |
| PNEUMA | "For what gives you the right to make such a judgment? What do you have that God hasn't given you?" | diakrino reinterpreted as "right to make a judgment"; adds "God" as giver |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Who sees anything different in you?" captures the diakrino sense (who makes a distinction, who sets you apart?) as a rhetorical question implying no one. PNEUMA's "what gives you the right" shifts from a question about distinction to a question about entitlement, which changes the logic. Adding "God" as the explicit giver, while theologically correct, goes beyond the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:8 -- Ironic tone rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Already you are filled! Already you became rich! Apart from us you reigned!" | Preserves aorist "became rich" and "reigned"; "apart from us" |
| LOGOS | "Already you have all you want! Already you have become rich! Without us you have become kings!" | "all you want" interprets kekoresmenoi; "become kings" for ebasileusate |
| PNEUMA | "You think you already have everything you need. You think you are already rich. You have begun to reign in God's kingdom without us!" | Adds "You think" twice (marking irony explicitly); adds "God's kingdom"; "begun to reign" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The exclamation marks effectively convey Paul's biting irony without PNEUMA's explicit "You think" markers, which remove the rhetorical force of Paul's apparent statements. PNEUMA's "God's kingdom" is an addition -- Paul simply says "you reigned." The irony works precisely because Paul states it as if it were true.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:9 -- "exhibited / displayed / put on display" and "sentenced to death / condemned to death"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "God displayed us the apostles last, as condemned to death, because we became a spectacle to the world" | apodeiknymi as "displayed"; epithanatios as "condemned to death"; theatron as "spectacle" |
| LOGOS | "God has exhibited us apostles as last of all, like men sentenced to death, because we have become a spectacle to the world, to angels, and to men" | apodeiknymi as "exhibited"; epithanatios as "sentenced to death" |
| PNEUMA | "I sometimes think God has put us apostles on display, like prisoners of war at the end of a victory parade, condemned to die in the arena" | Adds "sometimes think"; "prisoners of war"; "victory parade"; "arena" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Exhibited" and "sentenced to death" are precise. PNEUMA's vivid expansion ("prisoners of war at the end of a victory parade, condemned to die in the arena") is historically informed and homiletically powerful but goes far beyond the text. Paul's own metaphor is already vivid; it does not need embellishment. PNEUMA's "sometimes think" softens Paul's assertion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:10 -- Ironic contrasts: structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "We [are] fools on account of Christos, but you [are] wise in Christos; we [are] weak, but you [are] strong; you [are] honored, but we [are] dishonored" | "on account of" for dia; "dishonored" for atimoi |
| LOGOS | "We are fools for Christ's sake, but you are wise in Christ. We are weak, but you are strong. You are held in honor, but we in disrepute" | "for Christ's sake"; "in disrepute" for atimoi |
| PNEUMA | "Our dedication to Christ makes us look like fools, but you claim to be so wise in Christ! We are weak, but you are so powerful! You are honored, but we are ridiculed" | Adds "dedication," "claim to be," "so"; "ridiculed" for atimoi |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The parallel structure (fools/wise, weak/strong, honor/disrepute) is preserved cleanly. PNEUMA's additions ("claim to be," "so," "dedication") make the irony explicit rather than implicit, which weakens it. "In disrepute" (LOGOS) captures atimoi better than "dishonored" (EMET, too strong) or "ridiculed" (PNEUMA, too specific).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:11 -- "buffeted / beaten / often beaten"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we...are poorly clothed and are beaten and are homeless" | kolaphizo as "beaten" |
| LOGOS | "we...are poorly dressed and buffeted and homeless" | kolaphizo as "buffeted" |
| PNEUMA | "we don't have enough clothes to keep warm. We are often beaten and have no home" | Adds "to keep warm" and "often"; expands into multiple sentences |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Buffeted" for kolaphizo (literally "struck with fists") carries the sense of being repeatedly struck, which is more specific than the generic "beaten." PNEUMA's "don't have enough clothes to keep warm" and "often" add specificity not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:13 -- "scum / refuse / garbage" and "entreat / encourage / appeal gently"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "being slandered, we encourage. As refuse of the world we became, scum of all [things]" | parakaleo as "encourage"; perikatharma as "refuse"; peripsema as "scum" |
| LOGOS | "when slandered, we entreat. We have become, and are still, like the scum of the world, the refuse of all things" | parakaleo as "entreat"; perikatharma as "scum"; peripsema as "refuse" |
| PNEUMA | "We appeal gently when evil things are said about us. Yet we are treated like the world's garbage, like everybody's trash" | parakaleo as "appeal gently"; perikatharma/peripsema both rendered colloquially |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Scum" and "refuse" preserve the extreme degradation of the Greek terms (perikatharma may refer to ritual scapegoats; peripsema to offscouring/scraping). PNEUMA's "garbage" and "trash" are colloquially effective but lose the ritual and social connotations. Note: EMET and LOGOS reverse which Greek word gets which English term -- SOPHIA follows LOGOS's assignment.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:15 -- "countless guides / ten thousand tutors / ten thousand guardians"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if you should have ten thousand tutors in Christos, yet not many fathers" | myrioi paidagogoi as "ten thousand tutors" |
| LOGOS | "though you have countless guides in Christ, you do not have many fathers" | myrioi as "countless"; paidagogos as "guides" |
| PNEUMA | "even if you had ten thousand guardians in Christ, you do not have many fathers" | myrioi as "ten thousand"; paidagogos as "guardians" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Countless" effectively conveys the hyperbolic force of myrioi. The rendering of paidagogos is the key divergence: "tutors" (EMET), "guides" (LOGOS), "guardians" (PNEUMA). The paidagogos was a household slave who supervised a child's conduct and escorted him to school -- not primarily a teacher. "Guides" captures this supervisory role. "Tutors" implies academic instruction. "Guardians" is acceptable but suggests legal custody.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Paidagogos rendering noted as a significant lexical choice.

---

## 4:19 -- "talk / speech / impressive speeches"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I will know not the speech of the [ones] having been puffed up but the power" | logos as "speech" |
| LOGOS | "I will find out not the talk of these arrogant people but their power" | logos as "talk"; "arrogant" for physiomai |
| PNEUMA | "I'll find out whether these arrogant people just give impressive speeches or whether they really have God's power" | Adds "just," "impressive," "really," "God's" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Talk" effectively diminishes the Corinthians' logos (mere words vs. power). PNEUMA's additions ("just," "impressive," "really," "God's") intensify the contrast but go beyond the Greek. Paul simply contrasts logos and dynamis.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:20 -- "consist in / is not just"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the kingdom of God [is] not in speech but in power" | Minimal rendering with en + dative |
| LOGOS | "the kingdom of God does not consist in talk but in power" | "consist in" interprets en |
| PNEUMA | "the Kingdom of God is not just a lot of talk; it is living by God's power" | Adds "just a lot of"; "living by" expands; adds "God's" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Does not consist in" is a clear English rendering of the en + dative construction. PNEUMA's "not just a lot of talk" softens Paul's absolute contrast (Paul does not say "not just" but "not in"), and "living by God's power" adds interpretive material.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:21 -- "rod / rod to punish"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "With a rod shall I come to you, or in love and a spirit of gentleness?" | rhabdos as "rod" |
| LOGOS | "Shall I come to you with a rod, or with love in a spirit of gentleness?" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Should I come with a rod to punish you, or should I come with love and a gentle spirit?" | Adds "to punish you"; "gentle spirit" reverses "spirit of gentleness" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "With a rod" is sufficiently clear -- the disciplinary implication is obvious without PNEUMA's explicit "to punish you." "A spirit of gentleness" (LOGOS) preserves the genitive construction better than "a gentle spirit" (PNEUMA), as Paul is describing the quality of the spirit/disposition, not a spirit that is gentle.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA makes irony explicit rather than implicit | vv.8, 10 | "You think" markers; "claim to be" |
| PNEUMA adds vivid expansions | vv.9, 11, 13 | "prisoners of war at end of victory parade"; "enough clothes to keep warm"; "everybody's trash" |
| PNEUMA adds qualifying words not in Greek | vv.1, 5, 19, 20, 21 | "mere," "just," "impressive," "really," "God's" |
| EMET preserves Greek opacity | v.3 | "human day" for anthropines hemeras |
| LOGOS serves as consensus base | Throughout | Nearly identical to SOPHIA |
| Key lexical choices diverge | vv.4, 13, 15 | dikaioo, perikatharma/peripsema, paidagogos |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Chapter 4 features Paul's most intensely ironic and personal passage in 1 Corinthians, and the translator divergences reflect different approaches to handling irony. PNEUMA consistently makes implicit irony explicit (adding "You think" in v.8, "claim to be" in v.10), which paradoxically weakens the rhetorical force. LOGOS preserves the irony through punctuation and structure, allowing readers to discover it. EMET provides valuable raw material but at the cost of English readability (e.g., "human day" in v.3). The chapter's key lexical debate concerns paidagogos (v.15), where all three translators chose different English equivalents -- SOPHIA sided with LOGOS's "guides" as the best available option.

---
